payload.answer: Store the CSV as an external/raw table reference with provenance to the fixture, and create only a small set of structured cards for query-relevant subsets (e.g., specific ids or distinct id/name/value mappings), avoiding per-row expansion for 500-row repeated tables.
payload.decision: Store the dataset as an external/raw table reference (not as individual long-term memory facts), and only extract and store a small, query-relevant subset of rows/fields as structured cards, with provenance to the fixture.

entity_resolution.reason: No specific row values were provided; only a fixture identifier and a general schema pattern (id,name,value) are known.

payload.intent: Determine how the provided CSV table (500 rows) should be stored in the Brain personal memory system.

memory_cards.statement: For large repeated CSV tables (e.g., 500+ rows) with repeated columns like id,name,value, prefer storing the dataset as a raw/external reference plus metadata, rather than creating one memory card per row.
Extract only query-relevant facts (e.g., distinct ids or specific id/name/value mappings requested by future queries) into structured cards; leave the remaining rows unexpanded to avoid memory bloat.
Preserve punctuation and exact formatting when storing the original table reference; any derived fields should retain original values or record a transformation note.

details: Input contained no concrete CSV row content; decision made at the policy level based on table size and repeated schema pattern.

repair_options: If the user later specifies which rows/ids are relevant, extract those rows into structured cards with provenance to the fixture.
If the system requires searchable semantics, build an index over id/name/value externally and store only summary/index metadata in memory.
If exact formatting/punctuation matters for future tasks, store the raw CSV verbatim and record any parsing/normalization rules alongside extracted fields.
